data.title: BYD Company: A Better Bet Than Tesla
data.description:
BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY) benefited from surging delivery volumes in March and in the first-quarter as the company's EV products remain highly popular, both in China and abroad. The world’s biggest electric vehicle, or EV, company saw a rebound in deliveries in March, which is when EV sales start to normalize after the Chinese Lunar New Year holidays in January and February. Further, BYD just days ago released its expectations for first-quarter earnings which showed a massive upswing in profitability. Overseas exports are also surging, making BYD one of the most attractive EV plays in the Chinese market, especially when compared against a group of start-up companies in China. With Tesla (TSLA) also recently reporting weaker delivery figures for Q1, I believe BYD is a potentially more promising investment than Tesla.

Previous rating

I rated shares of BYD a strong buy in my last work on the Chinese EV giant, mainly because the electric vehicle company overtook Tesla (TSLA) in the fourth-quarter in terms of global deliveries. Additionally, BYD is priced at an extremely attractive valuation based off of earnings and is leading the industry group in terms of gross margins. BYD also recently announced that it is going to roll out its advanced driver assistance program to its entire fleet (including its low-cost electric vehicles) which is set to make BYD vehicles even more attractive to prospective buyers and could lead to an acceleration of its delivery figures.

Strong delivery rebound in March, BYD still leads the competition in terms of gross margins

BYD’s deliveries rebounded hard in the month of March 2025: the electric vehicle, which is known around the world for low-cost EVs such as the BYD Seagull or the BYD Seal, sold 377,420 new energy vehicles in the previous month, which includes both battery-powered and plug-in hybrid EVs, showing 24.8% growth year-over-year.

Battery-powered electric vehicle, or BEV, sales totaled 166,109 units, showing 18.7% Y/Y growth, while the EV company sold 205,310 plug-in hybrids, or PHEVs, showing 27.0% Y/Y growth. Both BEVs and plug-in hybrids sales bounced upward in March, showing a healthy recovery from suppressed January and February delivery levels due to the Chinese New Year holidays.

Additionally, BYD sold 72,723 vehicles in overseas markets in March, showing 89.2% Y/Y growth. BYD overseas figures includes electric vehicles that are manufactured in China, but shipped to other markets. March overseas deliveries were the highest number ever for the EV company in March 2025.

In terms of deliveries, BYD did about as well as Chinese electric-vehicle start-ups in March. The industry group I am using below includes Li Auto (LI), NIO (NIO) and XPeng (XPEV). While all EV manufacturers in the industry group saw a significant delivery upswing, the fastest growth in March was seen by XPeng which benefited from 267.9% year-over-year growth in March 2025 due to the success of its low-cost brand Mona.

Tesla, on the other hand, reported a 13% decline in vehicle deliveries in Q1 '25, which compares against a delivery growth rate for BYD of 38.7% year-over-year (only using comparable battery-powered electric vehicles). Tesla had by far the weakest delivery results of any major EV maker in the first-quarter.

March Deliveries

Y/Y Growth

Q1 Deliveries

Y/Y Growth

36,674

Source: Author.

BYD, now the world’s biggest electric vehicle company, submitted its expectations for its first-quarter earnings days ago: BYD sees 8.5-10.0B Chinese Yuan ($1.2-1.4B) in earnings for Q1. Based off of BYD’s Q1 ’25 earnings guidance, profits are expected to surge between 86% and 119% on a year-over-year basis. This growth is mainly due to surging shipping volumes in both China and in export markets.

What makes BYD an especially interesting EV play, in my opinion, and a potentially better EV play than Tesla, is that the electric vehicle company is already widely profitable and achieving a higher gross margin than Tesla. In fact, most EV start-ups are achieving significantly lower gross margins than BYD and only Li Auto, with a gross margin of 20.3%, beat BYD. The Chinese EV maker reported a gross margin of 17.0% compared to Tesla's 16.3% margin.

BYD is a better deal than Tesla

BYD is valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.86X -- based off of FY 2026 revenues -- which makes the electric vehicle company the cheapest large-cap EV play in the industry group. BYD is as cheap as it is because many U.S. investors are very hesitant to invest in Chinese EV companies for a number of reasons, such as ownership concerns, governance risks and the fact that the Chinese government inserted itself into the affairs of its largest technology companies in FY 2020.

From a valuation point of view, however, I believe BYD is the best large-cap bet on the EV market that investors can make because:

  1. The firm is the largest by delivery volume,
  2. It has massive delivery momentum in China and in export markets, and
  3. BYD is already profitable and is situated at the top of the industry group in terms of gross margins.

In my last work on BYD, I said that the Chinese EV firm could more than double its valuation based off of a fair value P/S ratio of ~2.0X which I still believe is very much achievable given the trajectory we are seeing in BEV and PHEV deliveries. A 2.0X P/S ratio gives BYD a fair value in the neighborhood of $86 per-share.

Tesla, which is America’s largest EV-focused automaker, is trading at a massively higher price-to-revenue ratio of 6.3X, while Rivian Automotive (RIVN) is valued at a forward P/S ratio of 1.7X. Chinese EV start-ups like NIO, Li Auto and XPeng are also significantly cheaper due to continual revenue and delivery ramps and profitability challenges (with the exception of Li Auto which is already profitable). The industry group average P/S ratio is about ~2.0X which why I believe BYD has massive upside revaluation potential going forward.

Risks with BYD

An escalation of the tariff war between the U.S., Europe and China could have dire consequences for BYD, although the company is not selling EVs directly in the U.S. Higher tariffs could lead to a shrinking of global trade which potentially could hurt expansion-driven BYD exactly at a time when it is ramping up its exports into other countries. In addition, U.S. investors are right about citing higher governance and transparency risks when it comes to investments in Chinese EV companies, so they may continue to stay on the sidelines and avoiding BYD as an investment.

Final thoughts

BYD is focused on ramping up deliveries for both its battery-powered and plug-in hybrids, in both China and in export markets. The EV firm has seen a steep increase in sales in both core product groups in March and in Q1 '25, and just submitted a solid earnings projection for the first quarter. Because BYD delivers more than 1 million new energy vehicles each quarter, the Chinese EV firm has such a big scale that it is already profitable… which sets it apart from other electric vehicle company, especially in the Chinese industry group which includes mostly start-ups. I like that BYD is trading at a very attractive valuation based off of revenue and I see investment advantages for the EV company given that it has a leading gross margin profile and the biggest delivery volume.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 10-Year Treasury Yield Snaps Back To February's 4.5%, Yield Curve Re-Un-Inverts, Mortgage Rates Back At 7%
data.description:
The aggressive bash-down by the White House of long-term Treasury yields and the dollar since January worked. Until it didn’t.

The 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.49% on Friday, back where it had been on February 20. It has snapped back by 50 basis points from the recent low on April 3 of 3.99% after a hard plunge.

By comparison: Starting in mid-September, the 10-year Treasury yield surged by 116 basis points to 4.79% on January 10, 2025, and there was no talk of an impending catastrophe. But now that the 10-year yield has risen by only 50 basis points to only 4.49%, and is once again barely above the effective federal funds rate (blue in the chart below), the media is generating piles of scary headlines about an impending catastrophe?

Starting in January, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent began actively bashing down long-term Treasury Yields and the dollar. Lower long-term yields would translate into lower funding costs for the US economy. And a weaker dollar would boost the economy by favoring exports (a positive in GDP) over imports (a negative in GDP). This was the explicit two-pronged effort by the White House to give the economy a boost. And the bash-down worked. Until it didn’t.

The bash-down caused the 10-year Treasury yield to careen down 80 basis points, from its recent high on January 10 of 4.79% to 3.99% on April 3. That was a lot very fast.

But there was an issue with this bash-down of the yield: Inflation is sticking around, and may be accelerating further, and the Fed is worrying about higher inflation rates going forward based on the tariffs, and is worrying that this inflation might become “persistent.” Future inflation rates might be in the 3% range or higher. The long-term Treasury market fears out-of-whack inflation more than anything.

But amid the bash-down, the 10-year Treasury yield went from 4.79% in January, which was already somewhat unappetizing in this inflationary environment, to 3.99% on April 3, which was outright gross in this inflationary environment. And investors lost interest at these low yields. Yields had to shoot up to where the buyers were, and so the 10-year yield snapped back after having been push down too far, and on Friday closed at 4.49%.

That higher yield brought out demand, including foreign demand

At the 10-year Treasury auction on Wednesday, there was very strong demand from indirect bidders, which include foreign buyers, including foreign central banks. The auction was described as “stellar.” The $39 billion in notes were sold at a yield of 4.435%.

Then, on Thursday, there was the “stellar” 30-year Treasury auction, where $22 billion in 30-year bonds were sold at a yield of 4.813%, amid very strong demand from indirect bidders which include foreign investors.

So the White House bash-down of the 10-year Treasury yield worked, until it didn’t, as the market ran out of investors that want to commit funds for 10-years at 4%. But at the current yields, investors scrambled to buy at the auctions.

The longer view going back to 2022 shows just how funny the fretting in the media and by the Wall Street crybabies is:

The 30-year Treasury yield closed at 4.87% on Friday, back where it had been on January 31, and below the near-5% rate on January 10, and well below the 5%-plus range where it had been in October 2023.

It has traded in a fairly narrow range since mid-2023, and the recent snap-back from the drop, when seen over this time frame, wasn’t much to write home about.

Yield curve re-un-inverted, still with a sag in the middle

The chart below shows the yield curve of Treasury yields across the maturity spectrum, from 1 month to 30 years, on three key dates:

  • Gold: January 10, 2025, just before the Fed officially pivoted to wait-and-see.
  • Red: April 11, 2025.
  • Blue: September 16, 2024, just before the Fed’s rate cuts started.

Rate cuts are on ice, and so short-term yields haven’t moved much and remain glued to the EFFR.

Longer-term yields have snapped back from the recent lows, but are still lower than on January 10.

As a result, yields of 7 years and longer are now once again higher than short-term yields, and that part of the yield curve has re-un-inverted. But there is still this sag in the middle, though it is shallower than it was two weeks ago.

In a Treasury market that doesn’t get aggressively bashed down by the White House, longer-term yields in this inflationary environment should be higher than they’re now. A 10-year yield of about 5% and a 30-year yield north of that would look about right, in my view.

Mortgage rates are back over 7%

The 30-year fixed mortgage rate roughly tracks the 10-year Treasury yield, but is higher, and that spread between the two varies.

Following the White House interest rate bash-down, mortgage rates fell with the 10-year yield, and now they too snapped back.

The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate on Friday rose to 7.07%, according to the daily measure by Mortgage News Daily. In the era before QE started, 7% mortgage rates were at the low end. In today’s inflationary environment, 7%-plus mortgage rates are somewhere close to reasonable.

The dollar bash-down

The White House bash-down of the dollar was also very effective, but unlike the 10-year Treasury yield, the dollar hasn’t snapped back yet. Though it will, sooner or later.

The dollar remains in its 3-year trading range and is higher than it was before that trading range. I had a little bit of fun on Friday with the catastrophist handwringing in the media over the dollar: OMG the Dollar Is Collapsing, or Whatever.

The dollar index (DXY), representing a basket of six currencies dominated by the euro and yen, dropped from 110 in January to 99.78 on Friday. But that drop was a lot smaller than the drop in 2022, and a lot smaller than prior drops, after which the dollar always bounced back. There were many years when the DXY was below 80. And at the current level, the dollar is still relatively high compared to where it had been in the prior 50 years. My article also includes a chart going back to the 1970s.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Tariff War Unlocks Dirt-Cheap Compelling Entry Points, Strong Buy Here
data.description:
TSM Is Even More Attractively Valued After The Recent Meltdown, Offering Interested Investors With The Rare/ Cheap Buying Opportunity

We previously covered Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) in January 2025, discussing why it remained a long-term Buy due to its leading N3/ N5 market share and the hyperscalers' promising AI/ data center demand growth over the next few years.

Combined with the double beat FQ4 2024 performance, strong FQ1 2025 guidance, and rich FY 2025 capex numbers, with it signaling a new investment/ growth cycle during the foundry's strategic global expansion, we had believed that it remained well positioned for long-term growth, supported by government subsidies.

TSM 1Y Stock Price

Since our recommended Buy rating between the 50-day moving averages of $197s or the 100-day moving averages of $189s, TSM has unfortunately breached even the 200-day moving averages of $173s to return most of its H2 2024 and Q1 2025 gains.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the bursting AI bubble by mid-February 2025, worsened by the intensified tariff war and the mixed macroeconomic environment, with it already triggering the deep market wide sell off by -21.4% between the 52-week heights and the recent bottom.

Before the recent tariff exemption on smartphones, computers, and other electronics announced on April 12, 2025, we can understand why TSM has pulled back as it has, with a similar correction observed in its semiconductor and foundry peers in varying degrees.

While the new US administration has previously exempted the import of semiconductor chips from the ongoing tariff war, it is apparent that there may be great impacts on the semiconductor sector ahead, since semiconductors are rarely used in its raw form.

With China facing a steep 145% import tariffs, Japan 24%, Germany 20%, India 26%, and South Korea 25% prior to the recent 90 days pause, we believe that finished electronic products including smart devices and data center components may get a lot more expensive moving forward, given that these countries comprise 48.5% of the global manufacturing output.

This is especially since the US "imported some $521 billion of machinery, $478 billion of electronics and $386 billion of vehicles last year, according to a Bernstein Research analysis," with the higher costs on finished products likely to eventually trigger an impacted demand on semiconductor chips down the line.

Well then, recent exemptions imply that the market pessimism surrounding semiconductor related companies may have been overly done, with it implying that the worst of the headwind may already be behind us. Naturally, this is assuming that China also lifts its 125% tariff import.

For now, while Alphabet (GOOG) had "reaffirmed the company pledge to invest about $75B to build out data center infrastructure," it remains to be seen if other hyperscalers may continue doing so ahead as well.

This is especially since the higher recessionary risks at 60% (up from the prior estimates of 40%) may trigger the hyperscalers' lowered capex guidance, depending on how things develop in the upcoming FQ1'25 earnings season in April 2025.

As a result, interested investors may want to tune into the upcoming earnings calls to better ascertain the impact of the ongoing tariff war, with it implying further volatility in the intermediate term.

On the other hand, numerous AI SaaS companies have continued to report the growing adoption of generative/Agentic AI across enterprises/ federal end markets, including Palantir (PLTR) and Salesforce (CRM), with it likely to drive the long-term demand for semiconductor chips, no matter the temporal macro/ geopolitical headwinds.

At the same time, TSM has already reported the promising preliminary FQ1 2025 sales growth by +41.6% YoY, while building upon the rich growth observed a quarter ago at +38.8% YoY and a year ago at +16.5% YoY, with it underscoring the durability of data center capex boom.

If anything, readers must note the tailwinds arising from the $500B Stargate project in the US, with the first data center set to be operational from H2 2026 onward, as numerous data center REITs continue to report their sold-out positions even for buildings under construction, including Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR).

While some of TSM's robust FQ1 2025 numbers may be attributed to the pulled forward sales cadence arising from "electronics makers had built up inventories in U.S. warehouses, preparing for possible trade disruptions," we are not overly concerned indeed, since it only proves the indispensable nature of the semiconductor industry no matter the ongoing macro/ geopolitical headwinds.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The same optimism surrounding the semiconductor industry has also been observed in the promising consensus forward estimates, with TSM expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +28.8%/ +18.8% through FY 2027, respectively.

This is compared to the original estimates of +8.7%/ +15.1%, the 5Y historical growth at +22%/ +27.7%, and the 10Y growth at +14.3%/ +16.1%, respectively, underscoring why the recent stock price correction has been unwarranted.

TSM Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that TSM appears extremely cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17.57x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.59x, compared to the 1Y mean of 19.52x/ 0.48x, 5Y mean of 22.50x/ 0.81x, and 10Y mean of 17.50x/ 1.08x, respectively.

The same conclusion may also be observed after comparing to its direct foundry peers, including Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) at the historical 5Y PEG non-GAAP ratio mean of 2.38x (prior to the memory correction in 2023) and Intel (INTC) at 5Y mean of 0.82x (prior to the PC demand destruction in 2022).

When comparing the Taiwanese foundry to the two other AI godfathers, including ASML Holding (ASML) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio mean of 1.35x and Nvidia (NVDA) at 0.68x, it goes without saying that there is an excellent margin of safety for those looking to add the market leading foundry with a global share of 67% as of Q4 2024 (+6 points YoY/ +15 from 2019 levels of 52%).

So, Is TSM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

TSM 5Y Stock Price

For now, TSM appears to have bounced after the worst of the market meltdown, as the bulls defend the $141s floor by early April 2025.

The selloff has directly triggered the narrower gap to our fair value estimates of $158.40, based on the FY 2024 adj EPS of $7.04 (+35.9% YoY) and the 5Y P/E mean valuations of 22.50x.

It has also contributed to the richer upside potential of +76% to our long-term price target of $266.10, based on the consensus raised FY 2027 adj EPS from $11.66 to $11.83, with it presenting a rare buying opportunity during the ongoing cloud super cycle.

While there may be growth headwinds in 2025, attributed to the ongoing tariff war as retailer "pulls the plug on China orders" and the tougher YoY comparisons, we believe that the worst of the market pessimism may already be baked in with a potential floor at $141 also materializing.

As a result of the rich double digits capital appreciation prospect arising from the ongoing market wide volatility and the improved margin of safety arising from the -33.2% stock price decline from the 52-week heights of $226.40, we are upgrading the TSM stock as a Strong Buy here.

While we concur that the "trade-related uncertainty is far from over," it goes without saying that the wider stock market (TSM included) is likely to rally back to previous heights, upon the resolution of the tariff war, and the lifting of market uncertainties, as similarly observed by +6.9% recovery from the April 08, 2025 bottom.

This reason is also why investors should simply buy “wonderful companies at a fair price” and holding through the massive market volatilities over a long period of time - in which “time in the market beats timing the market.”

Do not miss this rare dollar cost averaging opportunity.

On the other hand, readers must continue to note the risks related to TSM's investment thesis, attributed to the developing tariff war, as the new US administration "is looking at semiconductors and the electronics supply chain as part of upcoming national security tariff investigations."

This implies that the recent exemption may be temporary, with the rapidly changing policy environment likely to trigger a similarly volatile stock price movement - one that is only suitable for those with a higher risk tolerance and longer investing trajectory.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Apple Leads Q1 Global Smartphone Sales
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The iPhone had the largest market share in Q1, followed by Samsung. (0:15) Pfizer abandons weight-loss pill. (2:50) China halting rare earth exports. (3:36)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone took the top spot in global smartphone sales for Q1 2025, with a 19% share of the market, according to Counterpoint Research.

Sales in the U.S., Europe and China were either flat or declining, but Apple saw double-digit growth in Japan, India, Middle East and Africa and Southeast Asia.

The iPhone saw a +4% year-over-year rise.

South Korea's Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) came in second with 18% market share. The company had a slow start due to the late launch of the S25 series, but sales showed a resurgence after the launch of the flagship S25 and new A-series devices.

Senior Research Analyst Ankit Malhotra said: "Sales in January were particularly strong, with a subsidy-led demand boost in China. The momentum continued with major launches like Samsung’s S25 and iPhone 16e, but it turned quickly as economic uncertainties and trade war risks started mounting, especially towards the end of the quarter."

Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) (OTCPK:XIACY) took the third spot with a 14% share. The company was helped by expansion into new markets and significant growth in the domestic market, where the brand is seeing "a bigger and more ‘premium’ brand presence thanks to its successful foray into electric vehicles."

The fastest-growing top five brand, vivo, gained rose to fourth place, helped by high exposure to the China market and expansion into emerging markets. Meanwhile, OPPO rounded off the top five, and saw sales growth in India, Latin America and Europe.

Overall, the global smartphone market grew 3% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2025, according to preliminary results from Counterpoint.

Among active stocks, Crocs (CROX) warned its employees to closely manage expenses due to the higher supply chain costs the company anticipates due to the volatility around the trade war between the U.S. and other nations. Crocs produces about half of its products in Vietnam, which is a target for higher tariff rates.

In a company memo spotted by Bloomberg, CEO Andrew Rees told staff to remain prudent for the remainder of the year and be thoughtful about travel and which initiatives to prioritize. In addition, the board of directors' compensation committee has agreed to establish more realistic targets for the footwear maker's internal incentives plan.

Pfizer (PFE) said it would no longer develop its oral weight loss therapy, danuglipron, following a liver injury found in a participant who took part in a study for a once-daily version of the drug.

Citing data from a dose-optimization study for once-daily danuglipron, Pfizer said a single asymptomatic participant encountered a “potential drug-induced liver injury” after receiving the GLP-1 receptor agonist.

Bank of America has reiterated its top semiconductor stocks to own, a list that includes Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semi (TSM), KLA (KLAC) and Lam Research (LRCX).

Analysts said: “Even though electronics tariffs have been delayed and not necessarily canceled, we view the 1-2 month pause as a net positive as it allows for debate/discussion on this critical end-market that contributes ~50% of all chip demand.”

In other news of note, China has suspended rare earth exports in retaliation to recent U.S. tariff hikes. The New York Times says the Chinese government is drafting a new regulatory system for exports, and, while the policies are being framed, the shipments of the magnets, essential for the manufacturing of almost everything ranging from cars to missiles, have been halted at many Chinese ports.

On April 4, China imposed new restrictions on the export of six heavy rare earth elements -- metals that are refined exclusively in China -- as well as rare earth magnets.

Beijing produces around 90% of the world's rare earths, a group of 17 elements used across the defense, electric vehicle, energy and electronics industries.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman equity strategist David Kostin says; “The performance gap between high- and low-liquidity stocks has remained surprisingly stable despite recent market turbulence and worsening liquidity conditions,”

Typically, in times of market stress, highly liquid stocks are among the first to be sold as investors look to reduce risk quickly. This dynamic often causes these stocks to underperform during broad market drawdowns. But Kostin noted that the current environment is proving to be an exception. The high liquidity basket tracked by Goldman has demonstrated resilience, thanks in part to the strong fundamental characteristics of its constituents.

Goldman recently rebalanced two custom equity baskets, each with 50 stocks from the Russell 1000.

Among the most liquid stocks are the Magnificent 7, Visa, (V), AMD (AMD) and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A). Among the most illiquid TFS Financial (TFSL), U-Haul (UHAL), First Hawaiian (FHB), Seaboard (SEB) and TripAdvisor (TRIP).

Check out all 100 names.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPLV: 2025 Alpha, But There's A Price To Pay
data.description:
The Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) was above 60 last Monday morning. Fear gripped global markets, eventually leading to a selling spree in Treasurys along with equities. That all changed, at least for the moment, on Wednesday when President Trump posted on Truth Social that a 90-day delay in reciprocal tariffs would ensue. Then, over the weekend, and after a steep sell-off Thursday and Friday recovery, the POTUS announced key tariff exemptions on electronics produced in China.

Through all the macro drama, the Invesco S&P 500 Low Volatility ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLV) has outperformed sharply since the turn of the year. The fund is up 2% in 2025, beating the S&P 500 by many percentage points. Since my August 2024 analysis, SPLV is up 6.5%, a solid 600 basis points of alpha compared to the broad US large-cap index. I reiterate a hold rating.

VIX Soars to 60 Amid Market Uncertainty

According to the issuer, SPLV invests in an index tracking 100 securities from the S&P 500 with the lowest realized volatility over the past 12 months. Volatility is a statistical measurement of the magnitude of up-and-down asset price fluctuations over time. The ETF is rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly in February, May, August, and November.

Ranked No. 2 in its Sub Class, SPLV remains a medium-sized ETF with $7.5 billion in assets under management as of April 11, 2025. That’s up from $7.1 billion at the time of my summer 2024 analysis. Its annual expense ratio is modest at 0.25%, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is slightly above that of the broad market at 1.76%.

Share-price momentum is strong, particularly on a relative basis to the S&P 500, earning the ETF a solid A ETF Grade per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system—that's an improvement from a C grade three months ago. SPLV ranks well in terms of risk, but the fund can still exhibit volatility when the VIX soars—it's not immune to sharp and fast drops. But liquidity is robust—average daily volume is above 2.5 million shares, and its median 30-day bid/ask spread is just a single basis point, per Invesco.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star ETF by Morningstar plots toward the upper-left section of the style box, evidence of its large-cap value construct. Just 11% of SPLV is classified as growth, and there is just 2% small-cap access. Preventing me from upgrading the fund to a buy is its now above-market 21.2x price-to-earnings ratio. That’s a hefty price to pay given its long-term EPS growth rate under 8%.

SPLV: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

The sector breakdown with SPLV is interesting today. Whereas Consumer Staples was the biggest weight last August, Financials—not exactly a risk-off corner of the US stock market—is now the biggest position (along with the more traditionally low-volatility area of Utilities). Industrials, another cyclical sector, is 3rd largest. You have to go down to the No. 4 slot to find Consumer Staples.

The key to SPLV’s alpha so far this year is its relatively low 10.3% position in the Information Technology arena. That’s the S&P 500’s next-to-worst spot of the 11 sectors year to date.

SPLV: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, SPLV tends to perform well from March through August, while September has been the fund’s weakest month when scanning returns over the past 10 years. So, there are bullish calendar considerations today.

SPLV: Bullish Seasonal Trends

The Technical Take

Turning to the chart, SPLV has mixed indicators. Notice in the graph below that shares briefly dipped under the January low of $68.17 when the VIX soared above 50. Luckily for the bulls, the fund recovered sharply, now above its long-term 200-day moving average. What’s more, the bulls appear to be in control of the primary trend since the 200dma remains upward sloping with the 50dma above the 200dma.

But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart—it has printed a series of lower highs, even back when SPLV was testing its all-time peak above the $75 mark. I’d like to see a higher high on the RSI, along with a significant price thrust.

I also found that SPLV essentially achieved its measured move upside price objectives based on the height of the 2022-mid-20224 consolidation range. For now, long-term support is between $65 and $67, while $75 is resistance. Recall that last year, $75 was in play based on a breakout, and that played out nicely.

SPLV: Shares Holding mid-$60s Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on SPLV. After hitting my price target from mid-2024, shares are in a drawdown with a high P/E multiple. I am keeping my hold rating, but will monitor technical signals.

data.title: Cushman & Wakefield: It's All About Adaptability
data.description:
My rating for Cushman & Wakefield (NYSE:CWK) or "C&W" remains a hold.

I wrote about the company's third-quarter earnings and credit health in my earlier November 6, 2024 write-up. The current article looks at its ability to adjust to a new environment post-Liberation Day.

It's encouraging that C&W is strengthening its teams in the CMBL ("Capital Markets Business Line") and SBL ("Services Business Line") segments. Going forward, it has the potential to perform well in both good and bad economies.

Conversely, CWK lacks the flexibility to make different cash allocation choices depending on how markets evolve. This is because the company's financial burden is reasonably heavy.

I am Neutral on the stock, and this is supported by my mixed view of its adaptability.

New Hires Allow Company To Play Offense And Defense

Nearly three-quarters of C&W's FY24 sales were derived from the Americas region.

The macroeconomic conditions in the US will influence the company's future results to a substantial degree. Following the announcement of new tariffs by the Trump Administration in early April, it is uncertain if the final outcome will be win-win negotiated deals or a destructive trade war.

A CRES ("Commercial Real Estate Services") business should have a strong Capital Markets unit to ride on up-cycles ("offense"). It must also generate sufficient recurring services-related income to protect its downside ("defense"). In my opinion, CWK's key metrics for the prior year were disappointing in that respect.

C&W registered a modest +4% increase in its 2024 CMBL topline. The revenue growth rates for peers Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL), CBRE Group (CBRE), and Colliers (CIGI) (CIGI:CA) in the same segment were superior at 19%, 15%, and 9% (Source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

The company's SBL division also saw its fees decline by a low-single-digit percentage in FY24. In its 4Q24 press release, CWK explained that it terminated certain less profitable agreements and divested some of its peripheral operations for this business in '24.

In mid-2024, C&W recruited "David Bukovac" to lead its IFM ("Integrated Facilities Management") unit, which is part of its SBL segment.

David has previously worked in property management roles at JLL and Mondelez (MDLZ). This implies that CWK could tap on his experience to secure new wins in the F&B ("Food & Beverage") sector and take a leaf out of its competitor's book. This gave the company confidence to guide for a "run rate of mid-single-digit topline growth ('for SBL') by midyear" 2025 as per its analyst call comments.

Recruitment Headlines For The CMBL Segment

As per the chart presented above, C&W has added a number of capital markets-related professionals to its ranks since the beginning of the year.

These latest moves on the recruitment front are consistent with the management outlook (Source: earnings briefing) regarding a faster pace of topline expansion for its CMBL business in FY25.

CWK is now well-placed to survive in an uncertain environment. If the economy grows strongly, it has the talent to grab more deals in the Capital Markets space. Assuming that things turn southwards, the company has a new leader in the SBL unit to increase its defensive revenue streams.

M&As And Repurchases Are Less Likely With Capital Constraints

The stock prices for many listed companies, including that of Cushman & Wakefield will most probably be volatile and fluctuate in line with new tariff developments.

This represents an opportunity for businesses with robust balance sheets to enhance shareholder value with value-accretive capital allocation initiatives. I don't think that C&W belongs to this category.

At its FY24 results call, CWK stressed that it is focused on "steady organic" growth and "small-scale acquisitions". In a scenario where there are major rate cuts and a bear market comes to fruition, it's the time to gear up and pick up bargains. My take is that there is a low probability of the company engaging in a mega M&A deal to take advantage of such chaos.

Also, C&W doesn't have an existing repurchase program in place.

CWK's consensus next twelve months' normalized EV-EBITDA ratio has already compressed from 8.9 times at the start of '25 to 7.0x (Source: S&P Capital IQ) now. It makes sense for the company to buy back its own shares if the valuation de-rating gets worse.

I am of the view that it can't do either buybacks or inorganic growth transactions in a big way.

C&W's leverage metric ("net debt/EBITDA") was approximately 380% as of December 31, 2024. According to S&P Capital IQ consensus estimates, the company's gearing will only go down to a healthier 2.5x by the end of 2027. It doesn't have too much excess capital available, as it's bogged down with debt pay-down.

My Closing Thoughts

I still have a Hold awarded to C&W.

It has a mix of procyclical and defensive businesses to thrive in various situations. However, the company has a limited range of cash utilization options as it's highly geared.

CWK is also reasonably valued. This is based on the general rule that a stock's fair earnings multiple is roughly equal to its profit growth rate. The consensus FY24-28 EBITDA CAGR forecast for C&W is +8%, which is close to its 7x EV/EBITDA metric.

data.title: Barry Callebaut: Victim Of The Cocoa Prices And Working Capital Needs
data.description:
Introduction

About 1.5 years ago, I thought the worst was behind us for Barry Callebaut (OTCPK:BYCBF) (OTCPK:BRRLY) but I jumped the gun as the extremely high cocoa prices are still having a profound negative impact on the business case. The company remains profitable, but the higher inventory values were (and are) having a very negative impact on the company’s working capital needs. The company is guiding for a double-digit recurring EBIT growth on lower sales volumes, but the recovery is much slower than I had anticipated.

Barry Callebaut is a Swiss company and its Swiss listing (where it is trading with BARN as its ticker symbol) for sure is the most liquid. The average daily volume in Switzerland is approximately 26,000 shares. I will use the CHF as base currency throughout this article, as that’s the main currency Barry Callebaut trades in and reports its financial results in.

The first half of the year was fine, but higher working capital needs increase the net debt

During the first half of FY 2025 (the financial year ends in August, so the H1 2025 results discuss the situation until the end of February), Barry Callebaut reported a total revenue increase of almost 60%, and it goes without saying this simply reflects the higher cocoa prices which the company was able to pass on to its customers. This indeed means the gross profit didn’t increase (in fact, it decreased by approximately 1.5% on a reported basis) and after taking some of the other higher operating expenses into account, it was clear the performance of the company was relatively weak. One saving grace: whereas the company had to record almost 156M CHF in ‘other expenses’ in H1 2024, it only had to record 13.3M CHF in other expenses in the first semester of the current financial year.

The higher ‘other expense’ in FY 2024 was related to a restructuring provision, so the decrease in other expenses was generally expected.

The income statement above also shows the taxes were quite high, as the almost 69M CHF in taxes represents an effective tax rate of in excess of 69%. This is mainly related to some non-deductible expenses, which had a negative impact on the effective tax rate.

The combination of these elements is the main reason why the reported net profit was just 30.5M CHF, of which 32.1M CHF was attributable to the shareholders of Barry Callebaut (with about 1.5M CHF in net losses attributable to non-controlling interests). This resulted in an EPS of 5.85 CHF per share.

One of the main risks I now see with Barry Callebaut is the massive investment required in its working capital and inventory levels. As cocoa prices increase, Barry Callebaut needs to spend more money to retain a similar tonnage in its inventory. The massive impact on the working capital is clearly shown in the cash flow statement below, as the company reported a negative operating cash flow of in excess of 2B CHF, entirely caused by a 2.07B CHF investment in the working capital elements.

After also deducting the lease payments (25M CHF) and capex (123M CHF) and adding back the almost 15M CHF in interest received, it is clear Barry Callebaut had to deal with a massive cash outflow. This pushed the net debt level to approximately 5.8B CHF, coming from just 2.3B CHF one year earlier. Fortunately, Barry Callebaut had excellent access to liquidity as the bond markets easily absorbed the company’s newly issued debt, but it clearly shows how the high cocoa prices have an impact beyond just the initial margin compression.

I think the worst is behind us in terms of working capital requirements, and now it’s up to Barry Callebaut to manage its working capital. The worst thing that could happen to the company is a rapid decrease in cocoa prices, as that would result in an immediate impairment charge on the inventory levels if Barry Callebaut can’t sell its inventories at the current inflated prices. Looking at the futures market for cocoa, delivery in September 2026 is priced approximately 18% lower than the May 2025 futures.

The full-year guidance was adjusted as well

Given the challenges the company has to deal with, it has now adjusted its full-year guidance as well. The company now expects to see its sales volume decrease by a mid-single digit percentage, but still anticipates a double-digit EBIT recurring growth in constant currency. But of course, as Barry Callebaut reports on its financial results in Swiss Francs, the reported result may be lower, depending on the FX evolution in the second half of the year.

As a reminder, the recurring EBIT increased by 1.5% in local currencies but decreased when expressed in Swiss Francs. The recurring EBIT per tonne increased by 6.5% in local currencies in the first half of the year, but when expressed in CHF, the recurring EBIT increased by just 1.9%.

As a reminder, the recurring EBIT in FY 2024 was 704M CHF, after reporting 339M CHF in the first half of FY 2024. A double-digit EBIT increase in local currencies is great, but it’s still doubtful this will have a meaningful impact on the reported result in CHF.

Looking at the analyst consensus estimates, the estimates assume an EBIT of 770M CHF (note, this is on a reported basis, the recurring EBIT will likely be a bit higher), followed by an additional 10% EBIT increase in 2026 (again on a reported basis). That being said, I do expect these estimates to be trimmed a bit given the continuously difficult circumstances Barry Callebaut is operating in. In any case, the company has also committed to keeping its dividend at least unchanged at last year’s level of 29 CHF per share. At the current share price, this represents a dividend yield of almost 4% (subject to the standard dividend withholding tax rate of 35% in Switzerland).

Investment thesis

The consensus estimates are still calling for an EPS north of 80 CF in FY 2026. I am not quite sure that’s feasible, although I am confident in Barry Callebaut’s abilities to deal with the current fallout on the cocoa market. Let’s also not forget the company is a leader in its space and if anyone should be able to deal with the current situation, it’s Barry Callebaut.

While it’s never smart to try to catch a falling knife, I think this could be a good moment to start being contrarian. I hadn’t expected the cocoa issue to have an impact for this long, but Barry Callebaut will have to play the hand it’s dealt with. I currently have a (very small) long position in Barry Callebaut, and I am considering adding to this position, anticipating lower cocoa prices. I may try to achieve this by writing put options, as the contract size for Barry Callebaut is just 10 shares. For a P600 December 2025, the option premium is approximately 35 CHF per share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Moving Markets: From Tariffs To Recession
data.description:
The past few weeks have been wild, and the market has proven even more volatile than previously expected as the tariff drama unfolds. While a mild to moderate 5-10% pullback/correction in the S&P 500 (SP500) was anticipated due to the post-inauguration hangover effect and the "expected" tariff announcement, we witnessed a market meltdown instead.

SPX 1-Year Chart

In fact, the SPX's selloff up to and going into April 2 was just 10%, illustrating the potential for a double bottom around 5,500 support. However, instead of seeing anticipated "proportional" tariffs, the market received madness instead.

So, the market had a panic-driven meltdown, during which the U.S. stock market lost more value than in any other 3-day period before. I believe the actual peak to trough wealth drop may have been around $9 trillion in the three-day free fall following President Trump's tariff session.

While the message may have been well intended, the administration's delivery could have been much better. Also, I don't think the market expected China to react as strongly and defiantly as it did.

Ultimately, it's the bond market that decides things, and with demand for U.S. Treasuries seemingly crashing, something had to be done to stop the bleeding, which is where the 90-day tariff pause came in.

However, a lot of damage has been done from a technical standpoint as well as from a fundamental perspective. Therefore, we may not get the V-shaped recovery we've become so accustomed to in the past.

Where The Market Could Go From Here

The market became viciously oversold recently. We see this dynamic in the massive sale volume, the RSI hitting around 20, the CCI's negative 400 read, and in many other indicators.

There's also an unfilled gap around 5,500-5,600 resistance, which could get filled very soon. However, the million-dollar question is where will the market go after that.

We could potentially see more upside in the near term, but 5,800-6,000 could prove heavy resistance in the short term for the S&P 500. Therefore, I believe we should anticipate more volatility, exercise caution, and aim to stay nimble until more certainty returns.

In a more bearish case scenario, we could see another considerable pullback to retest the 5,000-4,800 level, and we may even experience another leg lower to roughly 4,600-4,400 if the probabilities of a recession continue to rise.

So, again, I want to highlight that despite the recently improved price action and some perceived light at the end of the tunnel on trade and tariffs, we are not out of the woods yet.

Probabilities Of A Recession Are Rising

Jamie Dimon recently said that a recession is a likely outcome. I don't always agree with Jamie, but the unleashed tariff circus, the crashing consumer confidence, the stock market meltdown, the bond market turmoil, and other worsening data signal that this time a recession may indeed come, 50%+ probability, in my view. Recently, Goldman Sachs (GS) increased its recession forest to 45% from 35%, upping its recession forecasts for the second time in a week.

Was The Decline "Engineered?"

After all, let's be honest. It was the new administration's policies that brought the stock market down to its knees recently. We all know that markets hate uncertainty, and the increased uncertainty is due to various policies, especially the White House's tariff policy.

Therefore, the logical question is whether this decline was "engineered." And actually, it makes sense. It's not that anyone wanted to see the SPX crash by 20% or tip into a bear market.

Nonetheless, it could be constructive to "reorganize" the trade agreements and get the other unpleasant elements out of the way before introducing "rehabilitating" policies like tax cuts, deregulation, and fiscal stimulus.

Perhaps even more importantly, the mini-crisis we're witnessing could enable the Fed to cut rates sooner and by more than anticipated. We see falling inflation, a stable but potentially weakening labor market, but perhaps most importantly, the Fed must hammer down the long end of the yield curve.

Bond Yields Surge

As Trump plays with fire, we've witnessed several very interesting events. First, long-term treasuries yields spiked recently, illustrating some real concerns in the market place. Now, whether the massive T sales were due to deleveraging or due to big sales out of Asia isn't clear.

30-Year Treasury

However, what is clear is that the bond market is sending a message, which is that the current policy adjustments are creating too much uncertainty. They need to be dialed back, or more considerable issues may arise in the bond market, which is the last thing the U.S. wants or needs.

We witnessed the 30-year spike by about 50 Bps after the meltdown event. Moreover, the dollar index cratered from a high of about 110 early this year to below 100.

Normally, we would expect to see investors rushing toward traditional safe haven investments like the U.S. dollar and U.S. treasuries. Yet, here, after the meltdown, we witnessed the opposite. International investors are seemingly dumping U.S. dollars and U.S. treasuries. This dynamic is likely what caused President Trump to back off on the tariffs.

Probabilities of Rate Cut Increase

While the probabilities for a rate cut are not as they were during the eye of the storm a week ago, they are still pretty high. Now, just for the record, when the "stuff" hit the fan last week, the probabilities of a June rate cut skyrocketed to nearly 100%.

Moreover, there was about a 40% probability that the Fed would cut by 50 bps, or twice by 25 bps by then. Now, we've seen the rate cut expectations come in a little bit, but there is still about a 75% probability that we will see at least one rate cut by June.

I Expect More From The Fed

Inflation continues to come down more than expected. We've seen this lower inflation dynamic in the recent CPI, Truflation, PCE, PPI, and other readings.

The CPI came in at just 2.4%, lower than the expected 2.5%, and much lower than last month's 2.8%. The recent PCE and PPI readings were also lower than expected. Moreover, Truflation continues to show inflation below the Fed's 2% target rate.

Truflation Is Around 1.5%

Truflation shows inflation at only around 1.5% here, and hitting as low as 1.2% recently. Truflation is typically ahead of the government's lagging CPI and PCE gauges. Therefore, it may put forth a more accurate representation of the current inflation dynamic in the U.S.

In other words, inflation is rather low here, likely low enough to cut now, and I would not waste any time if I was the Fed. The risk is now of inflation cooling too quickly, potentially leading to a partial deflationary environment, which would be a disaster for markets.

Rather than face the prospects of deflation, it may be better to risk the potential for some transitory stagflation. So, I think there should be much more urgency to cut here.

After all, mortgage and other key rates move with treasuries, and we recently witnessed a similar 50 bps spike in 30 year mortgage rates. Lending needs to be made affordable, and the market may require more liquidity.

Also, due to the recent bond market turmoil, the Fed can implement QE to hammer down the longer end of the yield curve, bringing long term rates down to acceptable levels as we advance.

Valuations Have Contracted

Due to the huge selloff, valuations have contacted considerably. For instance, the S&P 500 was around a 24 P/E ratio one year ago, and its forward P/E ratio is only around 20 here. This dynamic also implies that during the heart of the selloff, the SPX's forward P/E could have dropped into the 19-18 range, cheap considering most of the variables.

The Russell 2000s forward P/E ratio is down to 23 here, and the Nasdaq 100's is only around 24, which is really cheap. However, one factor that could make these ratios appear lower than they may actually turn out to be is the potential earnings decline phase.

There's still a lot of uncertainty in the air, and the upcoming earnings season may not provide the kind of clarity that the market needs. Many companies could report worse than anticipated earnings and firms could pull guidance in the current environment.

Of course, it is not guaranteed, but we could see a temporary earnings decline phase. While I remain optimistic due to the decrease in valuations, there is some concern whether the current estimates will be achieved.

The Bottom-Line

While we've seen a lot of volatility and the S&P 500 dropped by over 20% from its peak (peak to trough), we may not be out of the woods here so quickly. A lot of damage has been inflicted on the economy and the stock market.

With the probability of a recession at around 50%, it's not clear whether all the recession risk has been factored in, but it's likely that we will see more volatility ahead. Still, I despite the probability of near term turbulence, I remain optimistic, and have a 6,300-6,500 SPX for around year end/Q1 2026.

data.title: Rough Ride Exacting A Toll
data.description:
Preliminary estimates show that the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment for the US plunged to 50.8 in April - below forecasts of 54.5 - and the lowest level since June 2022. Consumer sentiment has fallen 40% since November 2024 (below since 1970, courtesy of The Financial Times).

At the same time, tariff announcements drove year-ahead inflation expectations to 6.7% (from 5%), the highest reading since 1981. See Consumer sentiment tanks in April on recession fears, and it’s not only consumers who are expressing angst about the outlook:

“Consumers report multiple warning signs that raise the risk of recession: expectations for business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets all continued to deteriorate this month,” survey director Joanne Hsu said.

Friday’s survey is the first major read on consumer sentiment since President Trump’s April 2 “Liberation Day” announcement of sweeping tariffs, which triggered a steep market selloff. The survey period ended April 8, the day before Trump announced a 90-day pause on some tariffs.”

According to the Bank of Canada’s Q1 Business Outlook Survey, business investment and hiring intentions have weakened considerably, with employment plans falling below pandemic-era lows. Thirty-two percent of firms surveyed expect a recession in the next 12 months, up from 15% in the previous quarter. March employment data reinforced these concerns with the job count falling and unemployment rising.

While most are banking on lower interest rates this year, a recent backup in Treasury yields has increased financing costs for the public and private sectors. The US 10-year Treasury yield leapt 63 basis points (BPS) from 3.86% on April 4 to 4.497% on April 11.

Companies borrow at a premium or spread rate above similar-dated Treasury bonds. Last week, high-yield spreads (companies with BB+ and worse credit ratings) rose to 461 bps, the highest since the March 2023 regional banking crisis (below since 2015).

So far, the Treasury market has been more attentive than the equity market. The last time corporate borrowing costs were this high, the S&P 500 was at 3800, some 29% lower than last week’s close.

With the US economy in decline, the Fed is expected to cut interest rates more in 2025, and both of those factors tend to weaken the greenback. Year to date, the US dollar has weakened sharply against major trading partners.

At the same time, as the US buys fewer goods from foreign countries, those countries receive fewer greenbacks and need to raise cash from other places to pay their bills. They have less to recycle into longer-duration financial assets, like stocks, corporate debt, precious metals, cryptocurrencies and treasuries. See The Dollar and the Bond Market’s Ominous Message for Donald Trump.

This is where liquidity needs can accelerate into a financial crisis. Some aggressively levered funds were forced to liquidate holdings after their positions went against them last week. And there will likely be more of that to come.

As asset prices fall, credit conditions tighten, just as fears about tariff inflation could dampen central banks’ reaction functions.

New Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said his office is focused on extending the term of the Federal debt to lower interest costs and extend payback periods. For that to happen, America needs the bond market to believe that inflation is under control and that lower interest rates are sufficient compensation for lenders.

As yields leapt into last Wednesday, President Trump announced a 90-day pause on additional tariffs, explaining that “People were getting a little queasy.” Queasy is an understatement.

Once entrenched, history warns that such negative financial momentum is difficult to reverse.

Disclosure: No positions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Jet Set Go: Why I'm One Of Boeing's Biggest Bulls
data.description:
Introduction

The aerospace industry has been one of the fastest-growing industries in modern history. Ever since commercial aviation went mainstream after the Second World War, it has enjoyed elevated secular growth from people's desire to go places.

Initially, growth mainly came from North America and Europe. Now, the biggest growers are Asian nations, which are experiencing a booming middle class. Meanwhile, developed markets are still growing consistently, as access to commercial aviation keeps improving.

Even better, this entire industry is dominated by just two players.

Imagine having a duopoly in one of the most favorable industries on the planet...

Despite this, The Boeing Company (NYSE:BA) is in what could become a period of seven consecutive years of losses, as it started losing money in 2019 due to 737 MAX issues, followed by the pandemic and a wide range of operational problems amplified by industry-wide supply chain issues.

As a result, over the past ten years, Boeing has returned just 16%, trading miles below its 2019 peak levels, while the S&P 500 has more than doubled during this period. Its European peer, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), has returned 168%.

With that said, there's light at the end of the tunnel.

For starters, the company's stock price has bottomed. Since September 17, when I wrote my most recent article on the company, it has remained flat despite a 5% decline in the S&P 500. That's not bad.

Moreover, it has won the NGAD contract, which is now named the Boeing F-47. As much as I wanted Lockheed Martin (LMT) to win it, as it's one of my biggest investments, it's another step in the right direction for Boeing.

Hence, I'll use this article to update my thesis and explain why Boeing remains a great risk/reward play for people who can stomach drama and volatility.

So, let's get to it!

Boeing's Big F-47 Win

On March 21, Boeing was officially awarded the NGAD (Next Generation Air Dominance) contract for engineering and manufacturing development. It was a competition between Boeing's Phantom Works and Lockheed's famous Skunk Works.

Boeing won based on best overall value, which is an approach that looks into items that go beyond cost, as the Air Force has figured out that going with the lowest bidder isn't always the best way to get value.

Anyway, what's interesting is that despite all the chatter that drones may make jets irrelevant, we're not seeing these fears in government spending. This makes sense, as I have often written that fighter jets are more than just flying machines that can shoot rockets.

This NGAD program is a flagship initiative for the Air Force, which has been envisioned as a "family of systems" centered on a manned sixth-generation fighter.

Essentially, the F-47 will come with advanced stealth, extended range, supercruise capability, and integration with drones and space assets to provide air superiority against peer adversaries like China and Russia.

In other words, it's a flying multitasker.

Having said all of this, the NGAD is the company's first win as a prime contractor since the 1990s, when its merger with McDonnell Douglas got it the deals for the F-15 and F/A-18 jets.

Now, Boeing needs to prove it is still up to the job, as it dealt with multi-billion-dollar cost overruns on programs like the KC-46A tanker and the Air Force One. This is on top of the many problems it had/has in its commercial segment.

The good news is that the NGAD contract is structured as a cost-plus-incentive-fee deal, which mitigates financial risk by allowing the government to cover overages while rewarding Boeing for meeting performance targets.

To add some numbers, the EMD phase of the contract (which Boeing just won) is valued at more than $20 billion. This includes options for low-rate initial production and a path to full-scale manufacturing.

As the cost of an F-47 jet is estimated to be close to $300 million, the total contract value could be hundreds of billions of dollars. This number could improve dramatically if foreign nations are allowed to purchase these jets as well. The F-22, for example, was not open for export.

The first production planes could leave the factory in the early 2030s, which means we're likely 5-10 years away from finding out just how successful this program can become.

For now, it's a big win, as it adds revenue stability and innovation capabilities (with DARPA support) that could result in more wins down the road.

This could include the F/A-XX, the successor of the F/A-18. After Lockheed has been eliminated from the contract, it could go to either Boeing or Northrop Grumman (NOC).

According to Defense News, this program is a major step for the Navy to increase its range and add new capabilities like AI.

F/A-XX will include new capabilities and technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, Rear Adm. Michael Donnelly said at the Navy League’s Sea Air Space conference. The upgrades will provide more battlespace awareness and improve how naval aviators make decisions.

Those technological advancements could help bring the Navy into a new era where piloted and unmanned aircraft operate more closely together, such as with the Navy’s planned AI-operated drone wingmen, known as collaborative combat aircraft, or larger, unmanned platforms that might come in the future. - DefenseNews

Although my personal opinion is that it is unlikely that a company wins two major deals in a row, it could add even more stability to Boeing's outlook, especially as growing defense operations tend to reduce cyclical risks of the entire Boeing business.

More Good News On A Path To Recovery

Last month, the company used the Bank of America Global Industrials Conference to update investors on progress in major programs. This includes the 737 program, which remains on track to achieve a production rate of 38 units per month, supported by inventory buffers for critical components like fuselages and engines.

This is good news, especially in light of the current tariff turmoil.

Additionally, thanks to resolved supply chain issues in the 787 program, it is looking to produce seven units per month next year, up from five units per month in 2025.

These operational improvements aren't just important in general, but because the company is sitting on a backlog of $435 billion, which covers more than 5,500 planes.

To further streamline operations, the company is working with the FAA to improve metrics like rework levels, traveled work (tasks moving between stations), supplier shortages, employee training compliance, defense escapes, and issue ticketing.

With regard to tariffs, it helps that 80% of its commercial and 90% of its defense procurement is domestic. Aluminum and steel account for just 1-2% of aircraft costs. On top of that, certain costs are hedged.

Moreover, by "mid-2025," Boeing aims to fully complete the takeover of Spirit AeroSystems, which is a former spin-off of Boeing and the world's largest producer of fuselages and other critical supplies.

According to the company, reintegration aims to improve safety, streamline decision-making, and align long-term investments, especially in 737 MAX fuselage production.

Culturally, Boeing plans to fully absorb Spirit employees "as if they never left," with immediate rebranding and leadership integration.

It also helps that the 777X remains on track for first delivery in 2026.

Debt Issues & Shareholder Value

One of Boeing's biggest problems is debt, which has soared due to financial losses.

At the end of last year, the corporation had $53.9 billion in debt, with nearly all of this being long-term debt.

In October 2024, Boeing raised $24 billion through a capital raise, which included a $10 billion credit agreement and plans to raise up to $25 billion through stock and debt offerings.

Moreover, last year, Fitch gave Boeing's new notes a BBB- rating. While this is still an investment-grade rating, the outlook is now negative, which means any headwinds could cause Boeing to lose its investment-grade rating.

The Negative Outlook reflects Boeing's heightened execution risks over the next 12 months to 24 months as it eliminates legacy inventory, stabilizes and increases aircraft production rates, navigates seasonal cash flow volatility, pursues additional corporate actions that should enhance longer-term operations and fulfills its commitment to repay debt and return to IG metrics. - Fitch Ratings (emphasis added)

That's the bad news.

Now comes the good news.

This year, net debt is expected to come in at $35.7 billion. That's gross debt minus cash. It would imply a 9.2x leverage ratio, which is abysmal.

However, that's due to very low EBITDA. Through 2027, net debt is expected to fall to $20.5 billion, just 2.3x EBITDA.

While Boeing may have become a 'mess' in recent years, it still has everything it needs to hit it out of the park, which Fitch recognized as well:

The company is one of two major global aircraft manufacturers with substantial diversification to differentiate it from its largest competitor, particularly given its large defense and services businesses. Fitch projects the company will continue to generate positive annual FCF throughout its rating case with potential to approach double digit billions by the end of 2027 as operations normalize and operating margins improve. - Fitch Ratings (emphasis added)

By 2027, free cash flow is expected to reach $9.0 billion, which would translate to nearly 8% of its market cap. Currently, it would give BA the highest free cash flow ratio of all major aerospace companies on my radar.

Applying its historical P/FCF ratio of roughly 15x, we get a fair market cap of $135 billion, roughly 15% above the current price.

To me, that's the base case.

We see something similar when using EPS expectations.

Using the FactSet numbers from the chart below, the street is looking for $6.86 in 2027 EPS, with 2026 being the first profitable year since the 737 MAX issues. Applying a 22x multiple, we get a fair price target of $150, which is a few dollars below the current price of $156.

While that may seem slightly bearish, I believe BA will get a much higher multiple once it becomes profitable.

If Boeing manages to become profitable next year and generate more than $9 billion in 2027 free cash flow, the market will reward this handsomely, as the next few years could see higher operating efficiencies, income from the 777X program, higher 737/787 output, and support from the NGAD/F-47 program.

Although it's fair to say that there are still substantial operating risks, I really like the risk/reward and would be a buyer of Boeing stock at these levels, if I did not already own five companies in this industry.

Takeaway

Boeing's story is messy, but full of potential.

After years of setbacks, the company is finally showing signs of a turnaround with major wins like the F-47 NGAD contract, improving commercial operations, and a roadmap to generate strong free cash flow.

Yes, risks remain, especially with debt and execution, but if Boeing stays on track, I believe the payoff could be substantial.

If I didn't already own five aerospace stocks, I would be adding BA here.
